chunk_summary: Ferritin-guided iron dose adjustment ensures safe and effective iron supplementation in preterm/LBW/ELBW infants post-discharge, considering factors like transfusions, inflammation, and growth needs.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of ferritin in guiding iron supplementation for preterm/LBW/ELBW infants.
Learn criteria for adjusting iron doses based on ferritin levels and clinical context.
Identify risks of over- or under-supplementation and strategies to mitigate them.
Incorporate ferritin monitoring into discharge planning for high-risk infants.
key_facts: Ferritin <35–40 µg/L with low or falling hemoglobin indicates iron deficiency; increase elemental iron to 3–4 mg/kg/day (up to 6 mg/kg/day in high-risk cases).
Reassess iron status 2–4 weeks after dose escalation.
Ferritin >300 µg/L without clear iron deficiency warrants reducing or holding iron supplementation, with reassessment in 4–6 weeks.
High ferritin may reflect inflammation, cholestasis, or recent transfusions, requiring specialist input.
Iron should be administered separately from large calcium/phosphate doses and not mixed into unfinished bottles.
reference_quote: Ferritin-guided adjustment improves safety when iron needs are uncertain (recent transfusions, inflammation, cholestasis, or rapid growth).
document_key_topics: Iron supplementation safety in preterm infants
Criteria for step-down or escalation of iron therapy
